questions.question: Why study a faded 2000-era standard like FIPA-ACL in 2026?
options: It is making a commercial comeback
The LLM agent-protocol wave is quietly reimplementing its ideas — knowing the heritage shows which "innovations" are reinventions and which old failure modes new specs will rediscover
It is required for regulatory compliance
MCP is a literal fork of FIPA-ACL
questions.explanation: MCP's tools/call, A2A's task lifecycle, and shared context stores are softer, JSON-native rehashes of FIPA decisions — with JSON contracts standing in for performatives and natural language for ontologies.

questions.question: What insight from speech-act theory underlies agent communication languages?
options: Speech must be converted to text before parsing
Some utterances don't describe the world — they change it ("I promise", "I request"); a message is therefore a performative (the action) plus content (what it's about)
All communication is ambiguous
Agents should communicate in formal logic only
questions.explanation: Austin's performative utterances, formalized by Searle into five categories, became KQML's operational model (1993) and FIPA-ACL's twenty standardized performatives (2000) — inform, request, propose, cfp, subscribe, failure, and so on.

questions.question: What do the non-content fields of a FIPA-ACL message (conversation-id, reply-with, protocol, ontology) represent for modern protocol designers?
options: Obsolete bureaucracy with no modern equivalent
Exactly the envelope you reinvent every time you bolt threading, retries, and shared vocabulary onto a JSON protocol
Encryption parameters
Billing metadata
questions.explanation: Seven envelope fields carry protocol concerns; one carries payload. Modern specs re-add these one at a time — correlation ids, reply threading, interaction protocols — rediscovering the same decision tree.

questions.question: Why did FIPA-ACL fade despite IEEE ratification and reference platforms like JADE?
options: A patent dispute killed it

Ontology overhead — parsing content required shared ontologies negotiated through years-long standards processes, while the web just used HTTP + JSON; its formal semantics also went unused in practice
Java became illegal in agent systems
It had no message format
questions.explanation: Agreeing on ontologies couldn't keep pace with the web. LLMs dissolve that specific blocker — natural language is the ontology — which is precisely why the FIPA ideas are returning in softer form.

questions.question: What is the relationship between FIPA's contract-net protocol and modern multi-agent patterns?
options: Contract-net was never implemented
Call-for-proposals → propose → accept/reject is the same shape as modern task-auction and delegation patterns — an interaction protocol the 2000s standardized that LLM swarms now re-derive
Contract-net is a blockchain protocol
It applies only to physical robots
questions.explanation: FIPA shipped interaction protocols (contract-net, subscribe-notify, request-when) as standard library items in JADE. The cfp/propose/accept-proposal performatives map directly onto how 2026 orchestrators auction work to specialist agents.
